name: Machine Learning Evaluation Metrics
description: Test your mastery of classification and regression metrics, from precision to ROC-AUC.

questions.question: What does a False Positive represent in a binary confusion matrix?
options: A negative instance correctly predicted as negative.
A negative instance incorrectly predicted as positive.
A positive instance correctly predicted as positive.
A positive instance incorrectly predicted as negative.
questions.explain: A False Positive occurs when the actual class is negative, but the model incorrectly predicts it as positive.

questions.question: Which metric is calculated as the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall?
options: Accuracy
F1-Score
ROC-AUC
Log Loss
questions.explain: The F1-Score balances both precision and recall by computing their harmonic mean.

questions.question: Why can Accuracy be a misleading metric for highly imbalanced datasets?
options: It focuses exclusively on the minority class variance.
It can yield a high score by simply predicting the majority class.
It requires an equal number of continuous features to execute.
It changes values depending on the classification threshold chosen.
questions.explain: If 99% of the data belongs to one class, a naive model predicting only that class achieves 99% accuracy.

questions.question: Which metric should be maximized when the cost of a False Negative is extremely high, such as in medical diagnoses?
options: Precision
Recall
Specificity
Accuracy
questions.explain: Recall measures the proportion of actual positives correctly identified, minimizing dangerous false negatives.

questions.question: What two quantities are plotted against each other on an ROC curve?
options: Precision and Recall
True Positive Rate and False Positive Rate
True Positive Rate and True Negative Rate
Predicted Values and Actual Values
questions.explain: The ROC curve plots the True Positive Rate against the False Positive Rate across various threshold settings.

questions.question: What does an ROC-AUC score of 0.5 indicate about a classification model?
options: The model possesses perfect classification capability.
The model performs no better than random guessing.
The model predicts only the negative class instances.
The model contains an equal balance of features.
questions.explain: An AUC of 0.5 means the model has no discriminative ability, performing identically to a random coin flip.

questions.question: If a model has high Precision but low Recall, what can be inferred about its behavior?
options: It outputs many positive predictions, catching most true positives.
Its positive predictions are highly reliable, but it misses many true positives.
It performs flawlessly on negative classes but fails on positive ones.
It predicts every single sample in the dataset as positive.
questions.explain: High precision means when it claims an instance is positive, it is usually right; low recall means it fails to find many actual positives.

questions.question: Which evaluation metric heavily penalizes confident but incorrect classification predictions?
options: F1-Score
Log Loss (Cross-Entropy)
Mean Absolute Error
Cohen's Kappa
questions.explain: Log Loss evaluates the predicted probabilities, penalizing wrong answers more severely when the model is confident.

questions.question: In regression tasks, how does Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) differ fundamentally from Mean Absolute Error (MAE)?
options: RMSE eliminates the scale of the target variable entirely.
RMSE penalizes larger prediction errors more severely.
RMSE ignores outliers present within the validation fold.
RMSE always yields a lower numerical value than MAE.
questions.explain: Because RMSE squares the errors before averaging them, it gives disproportionately high weight to large errors.

questions.question: Which mathematical components form the denominators for the Precision and Recall formulas, respectively?
options: Precision uses (TP + FN); Recall uses (TP + FP).
Precision uses (TP + FP); Recall uses (TP + FN).
Precision uses (TN + FP); Recall uses (TN + FN).
Precision uses (TP + TN); Recall uses (FP + FN).
questions.explain: Precision is calculated as TP divided by all predicted positives (TP + FP), whereas Recall is TP divided by all actual positives (TP + FN).
